schools.summary: A distinct second feeding; the crowd three days fasting, some 'from far' (Mark's detail); the disciples' renewed unbelief, borne with patiently; gather the fragments and be content.

Theophylact: Christ feeds because the crowd was in danger, not to draw food-seekers; allegorises three days = the baptized, seven loaves = the Spirit, four thousand = the four virtues vs the five thousand enslaved to the senses.

outliers.note: Allegorises the numbers — three days = the illumined/baptized, seven loaves = the perfecting Spirit, four thousand = the perfect in the four virtues.

schools.summary: Gill: Christ's compassion for both bodies and souls — teaching them three days and healing their diseases, his bowels yearning toward all.
Barnes: pity disposed to relieve (Psalm 145:15); Robertson on the parenthetic 'now three days'; Clarke on their expended provisions.
Ellicott corrects to 'some are come from afar' (perfect tense); Robertson: this item alone in Mark; Clarke on the danger of fainting on the long road home.
Gill: they were now fasting, with a long journey home, their hunger real and immediate.
Robertson: the disciples as helpless as before, not risen to faith after all they had seen; Gill: forgetting the first feeding; Ellicott on the 'lonely' wilderness.
Gill: they reason only from the visible means, forgetting the late greater miracle with less provision.
Gill: Christ begins from the slender means at hand, asking 'how many loaves' as before the first feeding.
The seven loaves the whole visible store; Christ multiplies what the disciples bring, not despising small means.
Ellicott: 'sit down' = recline; Robertson on 'brake and gave' (the giving kept on) — the bread multiplying in the breaking, ministered through the disciples.
Cyril of Jerusalem draws the eucharistic resonance with the Supper, where Christ took, gave thanks, brake, and gave.
Robertson: Mark lists the fishes last, served after the bread; Clarke: a detail not in Matthew; Christ blesses the fishes as the loaves, multiplying both.
Gill: the disciples brought the few fishes to Christ, who blessed and distributed them.
Ellicott: 'fragments' not 'broken meat'; Robertson on the overplus and the seven baskets (sphyrides, vs the twelve kophinoi); all filled to the full.
Gill: amazing that all were satisfied to the full from so little.
Wesley: the miracle proves Christ the true bread from heaven, almighty to support natural and spiritual life — pray, 'evermore give us this bread.'
Four thousand men, besides women and children (Matthew); the satisfied dismissal answering the opening compassion.
Theophylact (v.1): Christ sends none away fasting, willing all to be nourished by his grace.
Augustine harmonises Dalmanutha/Magdala; Bede: the sign sought was manna from heaven like Moses; Theophylact: Christ quits the incorrigible Pharisees.
Theophylact: he retires lest they make him king; the sign from heaven (sun and moon, hail) refused because its time is the second Advent, the first being full of mercy.
Robertson: 'began to question' = dispute, kept up; Matthew adds the Sadducees, the parties now joined against Jesus (cf. Mark 3:6); 'tempting' = ensnaring (Wesley).
Henry: obstinate unbelief always has something to say; Christ refuses the demand — if they will not be convinced, they shall not; a cause for lament.
Robertson on anastenaxas (a sigh from the depths, Christ resenting their prejudice); Barnes/Ellicott: Mark omits the Jonah exception, giving the flat 'no sign'; Lightfoot's rabbinic parallel.
Gill: he sighed over the hardness, malignity, and insincerity of those who sought only to ensnare him.
Gill: he left them as a perverse, hardened generation, with whom it was not worth discoursing — a judicial withdrawal.
Theophylact's principle: stay where there is hope of correction, depart where the evil is incorrigible.
Robertson: 'more than one loaf' a detail only in Mark; Ellicott links it closely to v.13; Gill: they forgot to take bread at Dalmanutha.
Theophylact: a special providence in their forgetting, that they might be blamed and brought to know Christ's power.
Barnes: Mark's 'Herod' for Matthew's 'Sadducees' — warned against all three; Robertson: the leaven of Herod (bad politics) and the Pharisees/Sadducees (bad theology); Ellicott on the repeated charge.
Theophylact: the leaven is their corrupt doctrine full of old malice, the Herodians teaching Herod was the Christ; Wesley: two opposite extremes.
Robertson: 'reasoned one with another' = discussion kept up (imperfect); they took the warning literally, of bread, not of doctrine.
Gill: recollecting they had forgotten provisions, they supposed Christ chided them for having no bread.
Robertson: Mark's six keen questions revealing Christ's disappointment; Gill: he knew their thoughts by omniscience; Ellicott: 'heart hardened' peculiar to Mark, vivid and emphatic.
Wesley: the strong words ('hardened,' 'eyes see not') are those used of the crowd, but here import only the present want of spiritual understanding, not judicial hardness.
The questions echo Isaiah (44:18) and Matthew 13, turned on the disciples; Gill: eyes of body and understanding alike, seeing yet darkly.
Gill: enlightened eyes that yet saw very darkly — a present blindness of the understanding.
The appeal to the first feeding (Mark 6:38); the disciples recall the twelve baskets exactly (the kophinoi), yet draw no faith from the memory.
The second appeal, to the feeding of the four thousand (Mark 8:1-9); the disciples recall the seven baskets exactly — a double witness to Christ's power they failed to read.
Robertson: the climax of the rebuke after all the explanation; yet Matthew records they then understood it was of doctrine, not bread — the patient teacher's lesson learned at last.

themes: how is it ye do not understand

schools.summary: Bede: the touch that heals the blind; led out of town; the gradual cure deliberate, to show the depth of human blindness restored step by step, and Christ's furthering grace.
Theophylact: Bethsaida's unbelief the reason for leading him out and for the partial cure — healing bestowed in proportion to faith.

outliers.note: Reads the unique gradual cure as a deliberate lesson on the depth of human blindness, restored only step by step.

schools.summary: Barnes: led out to avoid the crowd and the Pharisees, doing good without ostentation; the spittle a sign, not a remedy — the power attended the application; Clarke: it may have parted the gummed eyelids.
Robertson: a vivid description of dawning, incomplete sight, the single gradual cure; Ellicott renders the two verbs precisely and reads it as an acted parable of dim-then-clear spiritual perception.
Calvin: Christ asked for the disciples' sake, to show only a slight commencement of the cure; the man conjectures men from their motion.

outliers.note: Reads the half-restored sight as an acted parable of the soul's gradual illumination, needing a second touch to see all things clearly.

schools.summary: Gill on the repeated touch; Clarke: the variant 'saw all things,' and Christ sovereign of his graces, the small first mercy the beginning of fulness; Barnes develops the analogy of gradual spiritual illumination.
Robertson/Barnes: silence to avoid excitement and the Pharisees' jealousy before Caesarea Philippi; Clarke: the man not of Bethsaida; Ellicott: quietude and prayer for the right use of new sight.
Calvin: no miracle meant to remain buried, but concealed till Christ, having expiated sin by his death, should ascend to glory.

Bede: Caesarea Philippi (Philip's city for Tiberius); Christ asks men's opinion to try the disciples' faith, lest their confession rest on common report; Pseudo-Chrysostom: that they might praise him better than the crowd.
Theophylact: he leads them away from the Jews that they might speak the truth without fear; the guesses show many thought him John risen.

themes: whom do men say that I am

schools.summary: The popular guesses (John, Elijah, a prophet) all suppose a great figure of the past returned; Barnes on Herod's theory (Matthew 14:2); honourable yet wide of the truth.

Robertson: Mark's brief 'Thou art the Christ' carries the full idea; a serious test whether they still believed after the defections; Peter confesses for the Twelve; Clarke notes a variant adding 'Son of the living God.'
Robertson: tell no man he is the Christ, the time not ripe (Matthew 16:20; Luke 19:40); Ellicott: Mark's omission of the praise of Peter an evidence of the apostle's humility.
Wesley: three reasons for silence — not to encourage a temporal king, not to provoke premature destruction, not to forestall the resurrection's evidence.
Robertson: 'began to teach' = 'from that time' (Matthew), the confession the right moment; death at the Sanhedrin's hands; 'after three days' = the third day; the resurrection unheeded.
Cyril: Christ came to his passion willingly, foretelling it lest the world perish in its sins; Gill: now told plainly because they were established in faith.

themes: the Son of man must suffer

schools.summary: Robertson on parrēsia ('held back nothing,' repeatedly, Mark alone); Gill: spoken plainly and boldly; Peter takes hold of him (Wesley) to remonstrate.
Robertson: Christ wheels round, the disciples in view (Mark's touch), rebuking Peter before all who may have shared his view; Peter acts Satan's role, the wilderness temptation renewed.
Theophylact: 'Satan' = the adversary; Peter, unwilling Christ should suffer, savoured carnal affections and became his adversary.

themes: things of men not of God

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the call is free, not forced; self-denial = holding one's body and life as another's, bearing all for Christ; the cross even to shameful death, but for Christ's sake.
Bede: deny self = forsake the old and reach the new; the cross = abstinence and fellow-feeling; for persecution and for peace alike.
Robertson: 'and the gospel's' is Mark alone; Ellicott: it identifies Christ with the cause of the good news he died for (John 18:37); the paradox of two senses of life and save.
Gill: saving the present life by denying Christ forfeits the eternal; losing it for Christ saves it.

themes: save his life shall lose it
for my sake and the gospel's

schools.summary: Gill: the worldly bargain — saving life and gaining wealth by denying Christ — profits nothing at the issue, if the soul is lost.
The whole world weighed against a single soul and found too light; to win the temporal at the cost of the eternal is the most ruinous exchange.

themes: gain the world lose the soul

schools.summary: Gill: the whole world is no ransom for a soul; riches profit not in the day of wrath; once lost, it is past all recovery, for a man has nothing greater to give.
The sealing question: no price can buy back a forfeited soul, making the loss final and the warning absolute.
Barnes: ashamed of Christ's lowliness and doctrine; refusal to confess here means exclusion hereafter; the two comings contrasted — humility then, majesty at the judgment.
Clarke: the offence is a suffering, lowly Messiah; the world is ashamed of him still; he will disown before God those who disowned him before men.
Bede: the glimpse of coming glory given to strengthen the disciples to bear adversity.
